data.title: First Eagle Global Income Builder Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

Risk assets in general capped off a strong year with a solid fourth quarter. In contrast with recent trends, non-US equity markets led the way in 2025.

While the threats to market stability that have prevailed throughout the year were undiminished during the quarter, investors continued to embrace risk. The S&P 500 Index gained 2.7% for the fourth quarter and 17.9% for the year, while the MSCI EAFE Index returned a respective 4.9% and 31.2%. Non-US fixed income also saw success in 2025, with the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index returning 8.2% for the year while the Bloomberg US Bond Aggregate Index gained 7.3%. Notably, 2025 was only the third year in the past 10 that the MSCI EAFE has outperformed the S&P 500. Gold, meanwhile, surged 65% during the year, its largest annual return since 1979. 1

The US Double Bind Gets Tighter

Despite ample motivation for conservatism in an environment of pronounced macro, financial, geopolitical and structural concerns, risk perception in US markets remains low pretty much wherever you look. Equity market valuation multiples are rich, high yield spreads are tight and implied volatility is low. 2

While the post-pandemic normalization of certain macro factors has been encouraging, the country's fiscal settings remain far off-kilter. The US federal deficit remains historically outsized relative to the unemployment rate—as it has since the outbreak of Covid-19. 3 Normally, high unemployment rates and recession beget large fiscal deficits, as lower tax revenues combine with increased government spending. Conversely, low unemployment rates and robust economic growth typically support higher tax revenues and tighter fiscal policy, causing deficits to contract or even turn into surpluses. If the economy were in balance, we'd expect budget deficits of around 2% of GDP—not the 5.8% at the end of fiscal year 2025. 4

We believe this persistent deficit spending helps explain the decoupling of gold and Treasuries seen in recent years. The price of gold surged 65% during 2025—its largest annual gain since 1979—and has more than doubled over the past two years in an apparent acknowledgement of the double-bind facing US policymakers: Doing nothing to address the deficit could stoke renewed inflationary pressures, while taking action to curb it would likely increase the possibility of recession. More recent rallies in the prices of other precious metals like silver and platinum appear to reflect the same policy conundrum. 5

The US is not alone in this regard, of course, as fiscal deterioration has been widespread across both advanced and emerging economies. However, the US is among only a few key economies—alongside the UK and Brazil—facing twin deficits, and this combination of negative fiscal and current account balances represents an incremental risk that most others do not face. 6 The US current account deficit reflects an imbalance between savings and investment in the economy, which, by formula, must be offset by inflows of foreign capital into the US. The current account deficit is not necessarily a bad thing; the US has long been a popular destination for foreign investment, bolstered by the dollar's status as the global reserve currency. However, it does complicate efforts to consolidate fiscal policy.

Twin deficits are nothing new for the US, which has run them consistently since the early 1980s with only a few exceptions, the most recent being 2001. 7 More often than not, however, the fiscal deficit as a percentage of GDP has exceeded the current account deficit; efforts to bring the fiscal deficit to levels less than that of the current account deficit have the potential to bleed into the private sector, impacting free cash flow and causing corporate credit issues. 8

Beyond this fiscal reckoning is a question of how long the tailwinds that have supported both economic and equity market growth in recent years can persist. Chief among these has been the massive spending on the buildout of artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure. Spending on semiconductor fabrication and data centers on average has accounted for 0.4% of GDP growth annually since 2022, and the growth of technology investment overall has contributed nearly half of GDP growth in recent quarters. 9

Capital expenditures (CAPEX) by hyperscalers—companies like Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT) and Oracle (ORCL) that operate massive data centers supporting cloud computing—have grown rapidly over the past decade or so, at a pace far exceeding that of cash flow from operations. 10 As a result, capex as a percentage of cash flow from operations for these companies has grown from about 20% in 2015 to 70% today, and what had been very free-cash-flow generative businesses are now decidedly less so. 11 Spending on data centers and other AI infrastructure by hyperscalers is forecast to continue, but its current rate of growth is unsustainable absent some other sources of financing. To us, it seems likely to decelerate toward the growth rate of operating cash flow for these companies, which may represent an unwelcome plot twist in the market's AI narrative.

Finding Ballast Across Assets

Already-high geopolitical tensions ratcheted up a notch in early 2026 when the US took military action on Venezuelan soil to remove President Nicolas Maduro. The Trump administration has publicly offered a range of justifications for forcing leadership change in Venezuela—including the illegitimacy of the elections that brought Maduro to power, the country's role in the international drug trade and the seizure of US oil interests, among others. To us, one clear, if unspoken, goal of the operation was to check the influence of China and Russia on Venezuela and Latin America in general. Both nations have close diplomatic and economic ties in the region and staunchly oppose US dominance.

While escalations such as we have seen in Latin America in recent weeks are largely unpredictable, they are not surprising amid a geopolitical order in flux. We've spoken in recent years about the emergence of the Eurasian heartland, with authoritarian powers concentrated in eastern Europe and Asia—China, Russia, Iran and North Korea—growing increasingly aligned. More recently, the behavior of the US, long seen as reliable partner to like-minded countries worldwide, has led many to question the durability of its traditional alliances. Geopolitics is a complex system, and the current disequilibrium has increased the possibility of destabilizing left-tail events—be they in the Americas, Europe or Asia.

Perhaps unsurprisingly in this uncertain environment, the monetary value of gold has been reasserting itself. Earlier, we noted the significant increase in the gold price over the past two years, but this rally has merely aligned gold with its 50-year geometric average relative to the stock of US public debt while bringing it closer to its geometric average versus the S&P 500. 12 And though we're attuned to the risk inherent in such a sharp price move, we continue to highly value its strategic hedge potential given the fiscal and geopolitical dynamics currently in place.

Gold, however, is not the only source of portfolio ballast. Nor is cash. In fact, one of our focuses in recent years has been on building portfolio resilience through equities we believe offer ballast through their lower risk character. This is not achieved simply through higher allocations to traditionally defensive segments of the market like health care and consumer staples, though we do have meaningful exposure to these sectors. Rather, we evaluate stocks across industries from the bottom up in search of attributes we believe contribute to low correlations to the broader market, including strong balance sheets, high margins, diverse product lineups, long-lived assets and contractually obligated revenues.

We take a similar bottom-up approach to the fixed income sleeve of the portfolio. US Treasury yields remain somewhat above neutral policy rates offering value across the monetary cycle. Meanwhile Treasury inflation-protected securities still price inflation in line with the Federal Reserve's target. In a fragmenting global environment with elevated debt, we find inflation protection desirable. In addition, our bias toward higher-quality, shorter-duration positions gives us opportunities to recycle capital when we see attractive credit investments emerge in a market where spreads remain very tight.

Last year demonstrated that high benchmark correlations are not a prerequisite for strong portfolio performance, and we believe our benchmark-agnostic approach will serve clients well in markets that may be less accommodating going forward.

Portfolio Review

Global Income Builder Fund A Shares (without sales charge*) posted a return of 5.13% in the fourth quarter 2025. As of December 31, 2025, the Fund's equity allocation was 63.37%, comprising 41.47% international stocks and 27.90% US stocks. The Fund's 22.06% bond allocation included 16.67% in investment grade issues. The Global Income Builder Fund outperformed the composite index in the period.

Leading contributors in the First Eagle Global Income Builder Fund this quarter included gold bullion, Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF), and Power Corporation of Canada (PWCDF). Leading fixed income contributors included Mexico Remittances Funding Fiduciary Estate Management Sarl 12.5%, due 15-Oct-2031, Government of the United States of America 6.125%, due 15-Nov-2027, and Government of the United States Of America 6.625% due 15-Feb-2027.

As noted in the Market Commentary, gold bullion continued to rally in the quarter on the back of such drivers as geopolitical uncertainty, strong demand from central banks and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) and a dovish Federal Reserve.

Samsung Electronics is a global technology company and major manufacturer of diverse electronic components with a dominant presence in memory semiconductors. Results during the quarter reflect continued strong demand and shortage-induced pricing strength for DRAM chips and persistent demand from hyperscalers driven by AI infrastructure buildouts amid tight supply.

Power Corp. of Canada is a family-owned holding company with interests in a diversified lineup of insurance and asset management businesses in North America, Europe and Asia. Restructuring initiatives have taken hold; the company's subsidiaries are doing well and improved returns on equity reflect enhanced capital efficiency. With strong fundamentals and defensive characteristics, Power Corp.'s controlling family appears to demonstrate a thoughtful approach to risk management.

The Mexico remittances bonds referenced above are secured by a first lien on the remittance cash flow from the US to Mexico through the money-transfer business of Nueva Elektra del Milenio, the retail subsidiary of Mexican conglomerate Grupo Elektra. Although down slightly for the quarter—reflecting shifts in US immigration policy—remittance volumes remained robust and were bolstered by the steeper yield curve and currency translation gains from peso strength.

Shorter-dated US government debt performed well as short interest rates moved lower following widely expected 25 basis point reductions in the federal funds rate in both October and December. Markets are pricing in one to two additional 25 basis point cuts in 2026.

The leading detractors in the quarter were BAE Systems plc (BAESF), Douglas Emmett, Inc (DEI), and Texas Instruments Incorporated (TXN). Fixed income detractors included Oracle Corporation 5.95%, due 26-Sep-2055, Mellon Capital Iv 5.1313%, due 30-Jun-2043, and Government of Brazil 10.0%, due 01-Jan-2027

BAE Systems, the largest defense contractor in the UK, traded lower following several quarters of strong performance. The company is well positioned to benefit from prospectively higher defense spending throughout Europe in the face of uncertain military support from the US, a commitment to which was underscored by the recent agreement among NATO members to increase their defense spending. BAE's long-term government contracts generate recurring revenues that underpin strong backlogs and cyclical resilience.

Douglas Emmett is a real estate investment trust with prime commercial and residential properties concentrated in premium, supply-constrained Los Angeles and Honolulu submarkets, both of which have high barriers to entry. The office leasing environment in Los Angeles is still experiencing higher vacancy rates compared to before the pandemic, and political headlines about California and potential consolidation in the entertainment industry have also weighed on shares. We note that Douglas Emmett's premium office properties have a higher occupancy rate than the overall Los Angeles office market and its residential and Honolulu properties are performing well. We believe that Los Angeles is still fundamentally a highly attractive place to live and work, and rents and occupancy should improve cyclically in time.

Texas Instruments (TI) designs, manufactures and sells analog and embedded processing chips for vital functions in a broad array of applications. Management dampened expectations for the automotive and industrial markets during the quarter. That said, TI is well positioned for eventual cyclical recovery, in our view, driven by low customer inventories, continued reshoring and anticipated increase in its capacity. Management remains committed to maximizing long-term shareholder value, including dividends and share buybacks.

Oracle (ORCL) is one of the world's largest independent enterprise software companies, and the stock traded down in the fourth quarter after a very strong rally through September. Oracle has been aggressively building out its AI infrastructure, and its capital-intensive strategy has required the company to take on significant debt. Though it has a robust backlog with customers like Meta (META) and Nvidia (NVDA), markets appear concerned about the ultimate profitability of its data-center spending. Moreover, there was speculation that Google's Gemini 3 launch gave it the lead in AI search over OpenAI (OPENAI), with which Oracle has a strategic infrastructure partnership. Data center capacity is fungible, and we continue to believe Oracle should be positioned to capture recurring and sustainable revenue streams by providing services that support the use and adoption of AI.

Bank of New York Mellon (BK) is the world's largest custody bank, with a $2.1 trillion investment management business. Despite strong earnings during the quarter, long-dated bonds—including those cited above—were pressured by a steeper yield curve and broad macro-economic concerns. Fundamentals for Mellon remain sound as strong client acquisition and higher account balances underpin investment service fees. Management's ongoing focus on expense control has contributed to wider margins to better support debt obligations, and maturing debt securities have been reinvested at higher yields.

Brazil's shorter-duration sovereign bonds suffered during the quarter on heightened political concerns leading up to its presidential election in October 2026. In December, Flavio Bolsonaro (son of former president Jair Bolsonaro, now incarcerated) announced he would run for president potentially undermining the candidacy of a more market-friendly centrist candidate against President Lula—the standard bearer of Brazil's left, who will seek a fourth term. Volatility in Brazilian assets is likely to be elevated in the run up to the election.

However, yields in Brazil remain attractive relative to other emerging and developed markets, and the currency is undervalued.

We appreciate your confidence and thank you for your support.

Sincerely,

First Eagle Investments

Definitions

Gross domestic product (GDP) measures the total value of all economic output in goods and services for an economy. Free cash flow measures the amount of cash a company generates from operations after paying capital expenditures. Treasury inflation-protected securities (TIPS) are marketable US Treasury securities whose principal amount is indexed to the rate of the consumer price index. Exchange-traded funds (ETFs) are listed investment vehicles that seek to provide exposure to a benchmark, index or actively managed strategy. Federal funds rate is the interest rate at which depository institutions (banks and credit unions) lend reserve balances to other depository institutions overnight on an uncollateralized basis.

MSCI World Index (NET) measures the performance of large and midcap equities across developed markets countries. A net-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income net of withholding taxes. MSCI EAFE Index (NET) measures the performance of large and midcap equities across developed markets countries around the world excluding the US and Canada. A net-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income net of withholding taxes. Bloomberg US Aggregate Bond Index (Gross/Total) measures the performance of the investment grade, US dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market in the US, including Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, fixed-rate agency MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), ABS, and CMBS. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income. Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index (Gross/Total) measures the performance of investment grade debt from local currency markets worldwide. The multi-currency benchmark includes treasury, government-related, corporate and securitized fixed rate bonds from both developed and emerging markets. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income. Bloomberg US Corporate High Yield Bond Index (Gross/Total) measures the US dollar-denominated, high yield, fixed-rate corporate bond market. Securities are classified as high yield if the middle rating of Moody's, Fitch and S&P is Ba1/BB+/BB+ or below and is composed of fixed-rate, publicly issued, non-investment grade debt, is unmanaged, with dividends reinvested, and is not available for purchase. The index includes both corporate and non-corporate sectors. The corporate sectors are Industrial, Utility and Finance, which include both US and non-US corporations. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income. S&P 500 Index (Gross/Total) measures the performance of 500 of the top companies in the leading industries of the US economy and is widely recognized as a proxy for the US market as a whole. A total-return index tracks price changes and reinvestment of distribution income. Nikkei 225 is a price-weighted index composed of 225 stocks in the Prime Market of the Tokyo Stock Exchange. It is widely recognized as a proxy for the Japanese equity market as a whole. German DAX® Index measures the performance of the 40 largest companies listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange that fulfil certain minimum quality and profitability requirements. It is widely recognized as a proxy for the German equity market as a whole. CAC 40® Index is a free-float market capitalization-weighted index that measures the performance of the 40 largest and most actively traded shares listed on Euronext Paris.

data.title: MUU: Leveraged Trading Strategy To Amplify The Performance Of Micron
data.description:
The Direxion Daily MU Bull 2X Shares ETF (MUU) is a leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with 2x the daily performance of Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) shares. With demand for high-bandwidth memory (HBM) chips expected to continue to grow as an embedded component of advanced AI chips while NAND and DRAM fluctuate based on memory supply and demand, MUU can be used as a tactical strategy for traders seeking to amplify daily exposure to MU shares. Given the risky nature of trading leveraged strategies, investors should only consider MUU for daily exposure while employing strict risk management protocol in order to protect their investment.

About Direxion Daily MU Bull 2X Shares ETF

MUU was launched by Direxion ETFs on October 10, 2024. MUU has a net expense ratio of 106 bps, aligned with peer leveraged trading strategies. MUU exhibits strong liquidity with $1.05 billion in net assets and an average of $218.6 million in share value changing hands on a daily basis, at the time of writing.

MUU was designed to provide investors with 200% exposure to MU shares. MUU does this by investing in both MU shares and swap derivatives in order to gain the appropriate exposure to achieve 2x the daily performance of MU.

Swap derivatives were designed to provide the receiver with exposure to the returns on the underlying asset in return for a fixed rate. Swap agreements are settled at the close of each trading day based on the day’s performance, resetting the following day. As a result of the daily reset, MUU shares’ performance will reset on a daily basis, which may result in a compounding effect if held for longer than a single trading day. MUU shares will reflect the combined compounded performance of MU swaps and, to a lesser degree, the rolling performance of MU shares. MU shares account for roughly 9% of the portfolio weight.

The price of MUU shares can be impacted by a variety of factors that may lead to performance beyond the 2x target, including share price volatility of the underlying asset, trading volumes and liquidity, as well as the pricing of the swap derivatives. Though the 2x daily performance is the target, achieving it is not guaranteed.

Trading Strategies For Micron

Micron has historically been viewed as a cyclical company, meaning that revenue and earnings have gone through growth and declines over the years as the supply and demand balance of memory chips shifts from a constraint to a surplus. There are many factors that may influence the price of memory chips, including production across the major memory chip producers like Micron Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (SSNLF) and SK hynix Inc. (SXCH.F), as well as customer demand and inventory levels. For example, the 2026 market cycle is largely tied to increasing demand for HBM chips, which requires substantially greater wafer output when compared to DRAM, which has impacted DRAM production. As a result of the tightening market environment for DRAM and NAND, prices have faced increasing pricing pressure, which has benefited Micron and other memory chip fabricators. In general, I believe this has led to the positive momentum in MU shares and, in turn, MUU shares.

With the demand curve for HBM providing a more constant state of growth for Micron, I believe MU shares may either be undergoing an elongated growth cycle or decoupled from the cyclical nature of the industry. Given that the emerging popularity of AI is only a few years old, I believe it is too early to determine whether growth will remain as a growth trend or turn cyclical. The argument for a more consistent growth trend is that GPUs have an accelerated depreciation scale relative to general compute CPU servers, suggesting that GPUs may need to be replaced more regularly on top of new capacity. The contrasting approach would be determining the peak of the growth cycle and how much sustaining capital will be needed to maintain the required compute capacity.

Investor Suitability

MUU should only be used for daily exposure by sophisticated traders with a defined trading strategy. Given the increased exposure to MU shares and price volatility, mismanaging a trading strategy in MUU can result in substantial losses that may be unrecoverable. MUU could potentially be held for longer than a single trading day, though investors will add substantial risk to their investment outlay as they may be exposed to compounded returns to both the upside and downside as well as NAV erosion.

Additional Risks To Consider For MUU

MUU may expose investors to greater volatility risk relative to holding MU shares. Holding MUU for longer than a single trading day may expose investors to compounded returns, which could result in substantial losses that may be unrecoverable. Actively trading MUU may expose investors to increased short-term capital gains taxes on top of the stated expense ratio.

Given the heightened risk involved in trading leveraged strategies, I believe traders must set strict risk management protocols and adhere to sell discipline in order to protect their initial investment.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Final Thoughts

MUU can be utilized by traders to amplify their daily exposure to MU shares. MUU presents traders with substantial liquidity and trading depth, making the strategy appealing for active traders. Given the significant risks involved in trading leveraged strategies, MUU should only be used by sophisticated traders with predetermined risk management and trading parameters.

This article answers three questions about MUU:

  1. How does MUU amplify the daily movement of Micron?
  2. What risks accompany using MUU?
  3. How and when can MUU be used appropriately?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: PFO: Low Yield And Poor Performance Weaken The Case For This Fund
data.description:
The Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund (PFO) is a closed-end fund that investors may choose to purchase as a way of earning an attractive level of income from the assets that they already possess. The fund does reasonably well at the provision of income, as it has a 6.77% yield at the current share price, but there are several other preferred stock funds that boast substantially higher yields. Furthermore, this fund has been one of the worst-performing preferred stock funds over the past five years, and it is one of the only ones that delivered a negative total return over the period. The combination of a lower yield than its peers and its poor performance history might certainly prompt investors to consider other options, but we should never completely ignore any asset due to a poor recent history. After all, there have been situations in which an asset performed quite poorly for a long period of time before engaging in a powerful bull run that enriched its investors. Gold is a pretty good example of this, as its performance over the 2013 to 2020 period was very disappointing, but its performance over the past two years has been quite impressive. Bearing in mind that past performance is no guarantee of future results, let us have a look at the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund and see if it could be a reasonable investment opportunity today.

The Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund Compared to the Benchmark Indices

The name of the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund suggests that the fund’s portfolio will primarily be invested in preferred stocks and similar fixed-income products. The fund’s website supports this conclusion, as it states:

Under normal market conditions, the Fund invests at least 80% of its Managed Assets in a portfolio of preferred and other income-producing securities.

There are a few different types of securities that meet the definition of income-producing securities, all of which are various types of fixed-income products. These products include traditional preferred stock, trust preferred stock, hybrid securities that have characteristics of both debt and equity, contingent capital (a form of debt that converts to equity if the issuing bank’s capital falls below a specific level), and debt securities such as bonds. The fund’s most recent annual report states that it had the following asset allocation as of November 30, 2025:

Asset Type

% of Net Assets

Preferred Stock & Hybrid Preferred Securities

76.4%

Contingent Capital Securities

20.7%

Corporate Debt

1.7%

Money Market Funds

0.6%

We do, admittedly, see a few assets other than preferred stocks in here. After all, approximately 20.7% of the fund’s assets are invested in contingent capital securities (which are technically a form of convertible bonds), and 1.7% of its assets are invested in traditional corporate bonds. Thus, this is not a pure preferred stock fund in the way that the various index funds that track preferred stocks are. However, it is still primarily invested in preferred securities, so a preferred stock index works best as a benchmark for the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund.

As was mentioned in the introduction, the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund yields 6.77% at the current share price. This is somewhat higher than the current 6.19% yield of the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index (PFF). It may not be surprising that this fund would boast a higher yield than that of the index. After all, closed-end funds typically boast higher yields than index funds that track the prices of similar assets. There are a few reasons for this, including the following:

  1. Closed-end funds are almost always actively managed funds that frequently realize capital gains. Regulations in the United States require any regulated investment company (which most CEFs are structured as) to pay out nearly all of its investment income and net realized capital gains to the shareholders or suffer a tax penalty. The distribution of these realized gains effectively increases the yield of a closed-end fund when compared to the yield of an index fund that does not usually realize capital gains.
  2. Many closed-end funds employ leverage, which has the effect of boosting the effective yield that the fund pays out. PFO is no exception to this, as the fund’s annual report states that it had total assets of $215,636,568 as of November 30, 2025. However, the fund only had $135,036,568 worth of net assets as of the same date. As such, the fund had, as of that date, outstanding borrowings of $80,600,000, which it used to purchase the assets that are contained in its portfolio. The securities that the fund purchased with borrowed money still paid their promised dividends and coupons to the fund, but the fund only had to pay interest on the money that it borrowed. Thus, the difference between the dividends and coupon payments that it received and the amount that it had to pay in interest was additional income to the fund that it would not have had without the use of leverage.

The Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund does not only have a yield advantage over the domestic preferred stock index. It also has a yield advantage over just about any index that tracks the price and yield of either domestic or global fixed-income securities. We can see that quite clearly in this chart:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.87%

Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK)

6.55%

Vanguard Total World Bond ETF (BNDW)

4.10%

Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund (BNDX)

4.38%

J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Core Index (EMB)

4.91%

ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index (PFF)

6.19%

As of the time of writing, the two highest-yielding fixed-income indices are the ones tracking American preferred stocks and American junk bonds. The highest among these indices is the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index, which tracks the price and yield of speculative-grade bonds issued by American entities. The Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund has a higher yield than any of these indices, although admittedly, its yield advantage is not really that great when compared to the domestic junk bond index.

Many income-focused investors prefer to have some of their assets in fixed-income securities, such as preferred equities or traditional bonds. This makes a great deal of sense given that these securities have far higher yields than those of common equities. After all, as of the time of writing, the S&P 500 Index (SPY) only yields 1.05%. Clearly, a fund such as PFO has a substantial yield advantage over that index.

There are a few important things to keep in mind when comparing the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund to the indices above, as the closed-end fund has a few differences in terms of composition from the indices. Perhaps the most important of these is that PFO is a global fund that invests in both American and non-American securities. The fund’s website states that only 71.0% of its assets were invested in securities issued by American companies as of January 31, 2026:

The iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF, which was one of the most comparable index funds shown above, is solely a domestic fund that has all of its assets invested in securities issued by American companies.

The fact that the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund includes a 29.0% allocation to foreign-issued securities might give it an advantage in certain market environments. In particular, there may be times when foreign securities outperform American ones. This has been the case in the bond market since the start of 2026. This chart shows the year-to-date price performance of the Vanguard Total International Bond Index Fund compared to the Vanguard Total Bond Market Index Fund:

BND only includes bonds issued by entities that are based in the United States. BNDX is a bit different, as it includes no exposure to bonds issued by American entities. As we can see, BNDX outperformed BND year-to-date, showing that foreign bonds have outperformed American ones since the start of this year.

The price of preferred stocks usually exhibits a correlation in terms of price performance with bonds issued in the same country. After all, preferred stocks are quite similar to bonds in that both types of securities pay their owners a fixed amount on a regular basis that has no connection to the financial performance of the issuing company. Furthermore, neither of the securities benefits from the growth and prosperity of the issuing company. Therefore, both of them mostly trade based on interest rate movements rather than on economic growth or company-specific factors. Thus, during periods of time in which foreign bonds outperform American ones, there is a very real possibility that foreign preferred stocks will also outperform American ones. In such situations, PFO’s exposure to foreign securities could allow the fund to outperform an index that is entirely focused on American securities, such as PFF. However, in situations where American fixed-income securities are outperforming non-American ones, PFO’s exposure to foreign assets could drag down its performance relative to an index or fund that is entirely invested in American securities.

The website for the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund makes the following statement:

The Fund will invest, under normal market conditions, at least 25% of its total assets in companies principally engaged in the financial services sector.

This may not be surprising to investors who are reasonably familiar with other preferred stock funds. After all, most of them have a significant proportion of their assets invested in banks or other financial services companies. As I stated in a previous article on another preferred stock fund, the banking sector is by far the largest issuer of preferred equity in the world, which is largely due to international bank regulations. A bank must maintain a certain percentage of its capital as “Tier One capital,” which simply refers to a type of funding that is not simultaneously the bank’s liability to someone else. Common equity and preferred equity both fall under this definition, and as such, it is not uncommon for a bank to issue preferred stock whenever it needs to increase its capital rather than diluting the common shareholders through common equity issuance. In most other industries, it is cheaper to issue bonds in order to raise capital, so most companies outside of the banking sector logically prefer to do that.

The Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund currently has 53.5% of its assets invested in banks:

We can also see that 20.3% of the fund’s assets are invested in insurance companies. As both banks and insurance companies can be considered “financial services” companies, the fund clearly has exposure to this sector that is far above the 25% minimum that the website suggests. This is not necessarily a problem, however, as there is no place on the website that forbids the fund from having more than 25% of its assets invested in financial services.

It is difficult to tell how this compares with the ICE Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index due to the fact that the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF, which tracks that index, does not disclose the proportion of the fund’s portfolio that is invested in preferred stocks issued by banks. As we can see here, the website for that particular index fund states that 65.18% of its assets are invested in “Financial Institutions” as of February 10, 2026:

If we assume that “Financial Institutions” specifically refers to banks, then PFO is underweight banks compared to the index. However, if we classify both insurance companies and banks as “Financial Institutions,” then PFO is overweight to that particular sector relative to the index. It is also possible that the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund could be underweight to banks and overweight to insurance companies. There is no way of knowing for certain without a more detailed breakdown of PFF’s holdings. The most that we can say with certainty right now is that PFO has significant exposure to both banks and insurance companies, and potential investors in the fund should keep this in mind when determining how they can achieve diversification across their portfolio.

PFO Versus Peers in Terms of Yield and Composition

As we just saw, the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund boasts a higher yield than that of most indices that invest in preferred stock or fixed-income securities. This could potentially make PFO an attractive alternative to such indices. After all, individuals who purchase assets such as preferred stocks or bonds are usually interested in earning a high level of current income from assets that they already own. As higher yields result in greater amounts of income, such an individual might naturally gravitate towards assets that have the highest yields so that they can maximize their income.

The reason that PFO is able to boast a higher yield than that of the indices is due to its possessing certain innate characteristics that frequently result in closed-end funds having higher yields than index funds. The Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund is hardly alone in possessing such characteristics, however. Most of the fund’s closed-end fund peers possess the same characteristics, including active management, regular realization of capital gains, and the use of leverage. As such, the peer funds are more competitive with PFO in terms of yield than the exchange-traded index funds. In fact, most peer CEFs have higher yields than the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund. We can see that in this chart:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund

Fixed Income—Taxable-Preferreds

6.77%

Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund (PSF)

Fixed Income—Taxable-Preferreds

7.36%

First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund (FPF)

Fixed Income—Taxable-Preferreds

8.73%

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund (HPI)

Fixed Income—Taxable-Preferreds

9.13%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC)

Fixed Income—Taxable-Preferreds

9.08%

The median yield of this group of five funds is the 8.73% yield of the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund. This is a higher yield than that currently possessed by PFO, so the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Fund proves not to have an especially attractive yield when compared to its peers. In fact, PFO has the lowest yield that is currently available across its peer group. This certainly reduces some of the initial attraction that income investors may have felt towards this fund when they first saw its current yield.

The yield difference between the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund and the highest-yielding fund in the peer group (the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund) results in a fairly sizable difference in the incomes that investors in each of the two funds earn. After all, a $1 million investment in the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund will provide investors with approximately $67,700 in annual income. The same $1 million invested in the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund provides investors with approximately $91,300 in annual income, or $23,600 more than PFO provided. That works out to approximately $1,967 extra income per month from HPI, and that is certainly an amount that is difficult to ignore. After all, that additional amount of income could pay for a lot of things that you may wish to purchase during retirement, including travel, entertainment, or even gifts for your loved ones.

The relatively low yield of the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund compared to its peers has also been a drag on the fund’s performance. As we can see here, over the past five years, this fund has delivered a negative total return of 0.19%:

The Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund was the only fund in its peer group to deliver a negative total return over the period in question. As we can see, investors in all the other funds actually made money over the same timeframe. One big reason for this is that this fund suffered far more than its peers did over the 2022 to 2023 period, in which rising interest rates caused preferred stocks to fall from the high valuations that they had in the post-pandemic market boom.

As we can see, the shares of this fund fell further than did the shares of any of the peer funds over that period. This might have been due to the portfolio that this fund was running relative to its peers, or it may have been due to the high valuation that the fund had in 2021. After all, as shown here, the fund’s share price was higher than its net asset value over most of 2021:

Regardless of the cause, the share price of PFO fell more than that of its peers once interest rates began to rise, and it has yet to recover from that decline. When we combine that with the fact that this fund cut its distribution as the price and net asset value were falling and the fact that it has a lower yield than its peers, investors in it did not fare particularly well. The distributions that the fund paid out were not sufficient to offset the share price decline that this fund has experienced over the past five years. That is not true with any of the other four peer funds.

We can see in the chart above that the two highest-performing funds out of the peer group over the past five years were the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund and the Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund. While both of those are classified as preferred stock funds, their portfolios are somewhat different from that of PFO, and this may have helped their performance somewhat compared to the other funds. The John Hancock Preferred Income Fund, for example, has more than 40% of its portfolio invested in corporate bonds rather than preferred stocks:

Corporate bonds have a lot of things in common with preferred stocks, but there are also a few important differences. One of these is that corporate bonds have a maturity date, which specifies a date at which an investor gets the original purchase price of the bond back. Thus, an investor who holds a corporate bond from the date at which it was first issued to the date at which it matures will not suffer a loss in nominal terms (unless the issuer defaults). This is not the case for preferred stocks, as many preferred stocks do not have a maturity date (or if they do, it is so far into the future that it is unlikely to matter much). In addition to this, a company’s failure to pay a promised dividend to preferred stockholders does not push a company into default as it does if the same company misses a promised bond payment. As such, preferred stockholders enjoy fewer protections than bondholders and have no guarantees that they will ever get their initial principal back. This theoretically makes preferred stocks riskier securities than bonds, and this shows up in the trailing five-year performance of the two asset classes. As we can see here, the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index declined by more than the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG) did over the past five years:

Thus, the 40.70% allocation of the John Hancock Preferred Income Fund could have helped it to outperform the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund simply because the bonds prevented it from performing as poorly as a pure preferred stock fund would have.

Recent Performance Update

The last time that we discussed the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund was on May 18, 2025. As such, just under nine months have passed since we last looked at this fund. While there were a lot of things that occurred over the past nine months, many of them would not have had much impact on a fixed-income fund such as PFO. After all, as was already mentioned, preferred stocks derive no real benefit from things such as changes in corporate profits or economic growth. They simply pay out a dividend that is either fixed or floating based on some underlying interest rate benchmark. They are much like bonds in this way, which means that the most important determinant in their performance is the movement of interest rates in the economy.

Without a doubt, the biggest driver of interest rate movements over the past nine months was the Federal Reserve’s continued attempts to ease monetary policy and reduce interest rates. In this case, this came in the form of three 25-basis-point cuts to the federal funds target rate. The three cuts occurred during the Federal Open Market Committee’s final three meetings of 2025, which were held in the months of September, October, and December. This chart shows the effective federal funds rate since the publication date of the last article that I published on PFO:

We can clearly see the cuts to the federal funds target rate over the September through January period, as those are the periods in which the observed federal funds rate declined. As of today, the effective federal funds rate is at 3.64%, a significant decline from the 4.33% observed rate back in May of 2025.

Ordinarily, we would expect a decline in the federal funds rate to push interest rates lower across the yield curve and, by extension, push the price of preferred stocks up. It is indeed the case that the yields of preferred stocks in general declined over the period. The last time that we looked at the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund, the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index was yielding 6.63%. Today, the same index is yielding 6.19%, which is at least partially due to the index’s price increasing by 3.30%:

As we all know, the price of an asset and the yield that it provides move inversely to one another. As such, whenever the price of the index rises, we would expect the yield of the index to decline. This is exactly what we have seen over the past nine months.

As we have already seen, the bulk of PFO’s portfolio is invested in securities issued by entities that are located in the United States. As the index of preferred stocks of American issuers has risen, we would expect the share price of the fund to have risen as well. As we can see here, this was the case, as the share price of this fund has appreciated 7.32% since the last time that we looked at it:

As we can clearly see, the share price of the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund outperformed the domestic preferred stock index. This is somewhat what we would expect given this fund’s use of leverage. Leverage tends to increase both gains and losses, and as such, whenever the assets in the fund are rising in price, we would expect the fund’s net asset value to appreciate by more than the assets themselves are rising. As the share price of a closed-end fund exhibits a high degree of correlation with the net asset value of the fund, we would ordinarily expect a leveraged fund such as PFO to outperform comparable unleveraged index funds during periods of rising asset prices.

The fund’s outperformance versus the index was even greater if we look at it on a total return basis. Over the past nine months, the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund has delivered a 13.17% total return, compared to a 7.97% total return for the ICE Exchange-Listed Preferred and Hybrid Securities Index:

Once again, this is not surprising. PFO has a higher yield than that of the index, and total return is the combination of price appreciation and income. Thus, the fact that PFO delivered both stronger price appreciation and a higher level of current income resulted in it outperforming the domestic preferred stock index.

We should note, however, that some income investors might not have actually earned all of the total returns that this fund theoretically delivered. The reason for this is that the above chart assumes that an investor reinvested all of the distributions that PFO and PFF paid out over the period into additional shares of the respective funds. In addition, the above chart also assumes that the investor held their shares in some sort of a tax-advantaged account. Both of these assumptions may be unreasonable for a typical income investor who wants to use some of the distributions that the funds paid out to support themselves. In such a situation, the investor would not benefit from the compounding of the distributions over time, and the actual increase in wealth that they see in their accounts will be lower than the chart suggests.

About the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund

The website for the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund states that the fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of current income. When we consider that the fund primarily invests in preferred stock and contingent capital securities, this objective makes a great deal of sense. After all, both types of securities primarily deliver their investment returns through direct payments to their owners. These securities do not have any inherent link to the growth and prosperity of the issuing company, and as such, their potential for capital gains is quite limited. Indeed, in the case of bonds, there will never be any net capital gains over their lifetimes because bonds are issued and redeemed at face value. The same could be the case with certain types of preferred stocks, as the issuing company might have the right to buy back its preferred stocks at face value, regardless of the price that the investor paid for the security. As such, the investment returns that the securities in PFO’s portfolio deliver will usually take the form of direct payments that are made to the fund. The fund pools together the income that it receives from the securities that it owns and then pays it out to its own shareholders, net of the fund’s expenses.

The fund’s annual report (linked earlier) states that the fund received $4,282,405 in dividends and $10,014,370 in interest over the twelve-month period that ended on November 30, 2025:

This works out to a yield of 10.62% based on the fund’s net asset value of $135,036,568 as of the same date. Clearly, the fund is doing reasonably well at generating income from the assets in its portfolio, as this is a considerably higher yield than preferred stocks or contingent capital securities possess in today’s market. The reason why this fund is able to earn such a high yield is the leverage, which we already discussed.

The Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund does not, however, appear to engage much in asset trading activities. For the twelve-month period that ended on November 30, 2025, the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund reported a portfolio turnover ratio of 17%. This is similar to the ratios that it had in previous years:

FY 2025

FY 2024

FY 2023

FY 2022

FY 2021

Portfolio Turnover Ratio

17%

19%

10%

8%

13%

(All figures are for the twelve-month period that ended on November 30 of the respective year.)

These are pretty low turnover ratios, even for a fixed-income fund. The John Hancock Preferred Income Fund had a 35% turnover in its most recent fiscal year, quite a bit higher than that of PFO. The fact that the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Fund has such a low turnover could limit the amount of realized capital gains that the fund manages to earn. Indeed, during the most recent full-year period, the fund only had $239,323 worth of net realized gains, which is obviously not very much on a portfolio containing $135 million in net assets.

We should also note that some investors in funds such as these do not particularly like funds with very low turnover. The Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Fund had a 2.87% expense ratio for the twelve-month period that ended on November 30, 2025. That is arguably a pretty high price to pay for a fund that is using a “buy-and-hold strategy.”

Distribution Analysis

The Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund pays a monthly distribution of $0.0546 per share ($0.6552 per share annually). The fund has been increasing its distribution fairly steadily for a while now, and the current distribution is quite a bit higher than what the fund was paying at the time of our last discussion on it:

This string of distribution hikes might give potential investors a lot of confidence that the fund can maintain its distribution at the current level and possibly even increase it going forward. This may or may not be the case, and we should take a moment to look at the fund’s financial situation in order to make a more informed judgment call regarding the sustainability of the fund’s distribution.

According to the fund’s annual report, the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund had a net investment income of $8,239,256 for the twelve-month period that ended on November 30, 2025. That was not quite sufficient to cover the $8,437,491 that the fund paid out in distributions over the same period.

Fortunately, it does appear that this fund was able to make up for the difference through capital gains. For the twelve-month period that ended on November 30, 2025, PFO reported net realized gains of $239,323 along with $2,058,835 in net unrealized gains. Overall, the fund’s portfolio produced $10,537,414 in profits over the period:

That was enough to cover all of the distributions that the fund paid out over the full-year period. While this is encouraging, we should keep in mind that it can be dangerous to rely on unrealized capital gains to cover a distribution. This is because there is always the possibility that the unrealized gains will be erased by a market correction before the fund has the chance to realize them. In this case, however, it does not really matter, as the total of the fund’s net investment income and net realized gains was $8,478,579, which was sufficient to cover the distributions that were paid out (albeit barely).

Valuation

Shares of the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund are currently trading at a 6.74% discount on net asset value. This is reasonably in line with the 6.86% discount that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month.

The current price of this fund is fairly reasonable compared to its long-term average price. Over the past five years, shares of the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund have traded at prices ranging from a 17.38% discount to a 13.77% premium on net asset value. The average price over the five-year period was a 5.70% discount on net asset value. That is a better price than what this fund has today, and as such, the current price is actually fairly reasonable based on the fund’s historical range.

Here is how the current price of the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund compares with its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund

(6.74%)

Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund

(7.49%)

First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred & Income Fund

(4.24%)

John Hancock Preferred Income Fund

0.93%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund

1.84%

The median price of this group of five funds is the 4.24% discount on net asset value of FPF. The Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund has a current valuation that is somewhat better than the peer median level. While this may be nice, we should keep in mind that the Cohen & Steers Select Preferred and Income Fund boasts a better trailing five-year performance and has a more attractive price. As such, while PFO does not appear to be excessively priced, it could still be questionable whether or not it is worth buying at the current valuation.

Expenses

The annual report for the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund states that the fund had an expense ratio of 2.87% over the twelve-month period that ended on November 30, 2025. That figure includes the money that the fund had to pay in interest on the cash that it has borrowed in order to obtain leverage. If we exclude this money, the fund’s expense ratio is only 0.87%, which is very reasonable for an actively managed fund.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Flaherty & Crumrine Preferred and Income Opportunity Fund is a closed-end fund that aims to provide its investors with a very high level of current income. Its recent performance history has been quite disappointing, however, as the fund is one of the only closed-end preferred stock funds that delivered a negative total return over the past half-decade, and it has a lower yield than many of its peers. To my mind, this does not make the fund an especially attractive choice for anyone who is seeking to earn income from an investment in preferred stocks, as higher income and potentially less risk can be found elsewhere. One good thing about this fund, though, is that it appears to be fully covering its distribution solely with net investment income and net realized gains, which is generally a better policy than relying on unrealized gains to offset distributions.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Pentair: Peaking Pricing Power Could Be A Problem
data.description:
I would consider Pentair plc (PNR) a quality company in general, but there are some issues here that need to improve if the shares are going to break out of a fairly nondescript long-term performance pattern. Management has done well on margins, but free cash flow has only kept up with revenue growth, volume hasn’t really grown at all since 2021, and the company has lagged its industrial peers on organic growth every quarter since 2022.

I was neutral on Pentair with my last article and thought it was worth considering on a pullback. Investors indeed got that pullback and would be up more than 25% from those lows. However, the shares have risen only about 15% overall, lagging an industrial sector that has really perked up lately on expectations of stronger short-cycle recoveries.

Pentair isn’t really short-cycle-sensitive, and I’m worried that the company will have to pull back some on pricing in 2026 after years of relatively aggressive moves. I’m also concerned that important industrial markets like agriculture, food service, and mining aren’t really going to help too much. With the shares around my fair value estimate, I can see some appeal as other industrials get more expensive, but I’d like to see better organic growth to complement what management has done with margin improvements.

Weak Volumes But Some Margin Improvement In Q4

On balance, this wasn’t a particularly exciting quarter for Pentair, as the company’s organic growth once again lags the broader sector and margin improvement is becoming more of an expectation than a real sentiment driver.

Revenue rose a little less than 4% this quarter, good enough for a small beat versus Street expectations, but once again weaker than the broader industrial space (which is seeing around 4.5% growth on average).

Gross margin improved by 160bp to 40.4% as the company continued to use price to offset tariff pressures, and this drove the earnings upside for the quarter. Adjusted operating income rose 9%, with margin up 90bp to 24.7%, as SG&A spending was a little higher than expected. Segment-level profits rose almost 9% with a similar 90bp margin improvement to 27.1%.

The Pool business saw 9% core revenue growth, virtually all of which came from price. Segment profits improved 11%, with margin down 20bp to 33.6%.

Water Solutions revenue declined 4% on a 9% volume contraction, with Commercial down 4% on a core basis and Residential down 1%. Segment profit was flat, with margin up 130bp to 23.9%.

Flow revenue rose 4%, with 4% growth in Residential, 12% growth in Commercial, and 12% growth in Industrial. Segment-level profit rose 22%, with margin up 240bp to 22.8%.

Sluggish Guidance And Some Cloudy End-Market Outlooks

Pentair definitely disappointed the Street in a quarter where beat-and-raise guidance has been more the norm. Management’s revenue guidance midpoint was about 1% below the Street, and investors were disappointed in the outlook for flat volumes and more caution on pricing (up 3% to 4%, largely offsetting inflation and tariffs). With EPS guidance also about 1% below the Street at the midpoint, it would seem that margin leverage has slowed as a driver of outperformance.

Looking at 2026, I don’t see a lot of markets that stand out as clear positive drivers.

Pentair has done well in Pool on a consistent basis, but even acknowledging efforts in areas like product development (including convenience-enabling automation, AI, and IoT), I’m concerned that the company has been perhaps too aggressive on pricing. The “strained consumer thesis” is pretty well accepted at this point, and the housing market remains relatively soft, which doesn’t bode well for volumes.

An improving outlook for non-resi construction should drive some momentum in areas like fire suppression, but the restaurant capex environment is still poor, and that’s going to weigh on an important end-market.

Industrial markets are more mixed.

While Pentair’s products fit well with an ongoing drive toward automation in mining, overall mining capex spending is looking a little iffy at this point, though commodity prices are at a level that should encourage volume/capacity growth.

Food and beverage should be an area of relative strength. Membrane and filtration technology offers leverage to the current trend toward higher-protein offerings, as protein sources like whey are filtered from milk processing waste streams, but this is a small and emerging business for Pentair on a relative basis. Agriculture is still not looking all that healthy, as weak crop prices disincentivize hardware upgrades (like irrigation) and increased use of productivity chemicals.

The Outlook

My biggest complaint about Pentair may simply be that the positives are well known and appear to be flattening out. Management’s 80/20 continuous improvement initiative has helped lift EBITDA margin from below 20% to over 26% in just three years, but I expect the pace of improvement to slow in the coming years. Likewise, a mid-teens ROIC is comparatively pretty good, but further improvement appears to be slowing, and free cash flow generation has really only been keeping pace with revenue over the last five years (and lagging over the last 10 years).

What this suggests to me is that management may need to be more active in areas like R&D and product vitality. Minimal volume growth since 2021 is a concern, and if margin leverage is starting to peter out, organic growth is going to become a more significant driver. In some ways this reminds me of what got 3M (MMM) into trouble—the company built an extremely strong margin machine but dropped the ball on innovation and saw volumes erode over time as a result.

I’m at the lower end of management’s guidance for 2026 with 3% revenue growth, with some upside if housing and food service markets recover more in the year. I’m expecting around 4% growth over the next three years, and Pentair isn’t really all that well-leveraged to the short-cycle recovery theme. Longer term, I expected annualized revenue of around 4%.

I do expect further EBITDA margin improvement, but at a slowing pace, with around 30% margin in five years and around three points of operating margin leverage over the next three years. With improvements in working capital efficiency, this should drive free cash flow margins into the low-to-mid-20%s, driving around 8% of long-term FCF growth.

Between discounted cash flow and margin/return/growth-driven EV/EBITDA (a forward multiple of 16x), I think Pentair shares are more or less fairly valued with near-term undervaluation of around 5% and long-term annualized total return potential of around 7%-8%.

The Bottom Line

With limited leverage to short-cycle recoveries and a comparatively weak track record where volume and organic growth are concerned, I’m not sure what would drive a strong rerating of Pentair shares other than perhaps some bargain-shopping. Improving residential and commercial markets could help, and the company could deploy more capital into M&A, but as is, this looks like a pretty archetypical “it’s okay, I guess” investment story.

data.title: Wasatch Small Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

Small cap value stocks delivered a solid gain in the fourth quarter and finished the year with a double-digit total return. Investors appeared encouraged by falling interest rates and an improving economic outlook for 2026, propelling the Russell 2000 Value® Index—the Fund’s benchmark—to a series of all-time highs through mid-December. Despite the positive showing for the broader asset class, the Wasatch Small Cap Value Fund—Investor Class (WMCVX) declined -3.88% and underperformed the benchmark, which gained 3.26%. The primary reason for the shortfall was our tilt toward higher-quality companies, together with our avoidance of the types of momentum stocks that led the rally.

Our emphasis on quality was the most notable driver of the Fund’s underperformance over the full year, as well. The investment backdrop was characterized by persistent strength in stocks on the lower end of the quality spectrum, as gauged by metrics such as profitability, return on equity and free cash flow, among others. Companies without current earnings performed particularly well during the year. In this sense, our underperformance for the quarter and for all of 2025 was largely a function of the stocks the Fund didn’t own rather than those it held. The phenomenon was perhaps most pronounced in the health-care sector, where highly speculative biotechnology stocks—stocks to which the Fund had no exposure—led the way at the expense of the quality companies held by the Fund. Although our emphasis on quality has weighed on relative performance for several quarters, it has been a key factor in the Fund’s long-term outperformance. We therefore remain true to our patient and long-term approach, rather than attempting to chase transitory market trends.

Details of the quarter

Weakness among holdings in the consumer-discretionary sector weighed on Fund performance in the fourth quarter. For-profit education provider Grand Canyon Education, Inc. (LOPE) was a key detractor, as were Ollie’s Bargain Outlet Holdings, Inc. (OLLI), and Frontdoor, Inc. (FTDR). These stocks, while representing a broad range of businesses, have a common theme: all had performed very well through the first nine months of the year but sold off following earnings reports that were insufficient to fuel continued momentum. Believing the fundamentals of these companies remain strong, we maintain long-term positions.

The health-care sector was also a source of weakness, with Certara, Inc. (CERT)—a supplier to biotechnology development firms—weighing on results for the second consecutive quarter. Investors have been focused on the short-term headwinds caused by the weak funding environment for biotechnology firms, but we remain confident in the company’s longer-term fundamentals and market position. The Fund’s positioning in financials was a further headwind, due in part to a limited weighting in the types of small banks that outperformed, as well as weakness in Shift4 Payments, Inc. (FOUR). The stock came under pressure from worries about potential softness in the consumer sector, obscuring what we believe is more favorable longer-term outlook.

On the positive side, the double-digit return of our group of stocks in the information-technology sector partially offset underperformance in other sectors. Fabrinet (FN), an outsourced provider of optical equipment used in datacenters, gained ground and built on its robust year-to-date return. The company, which is seen as a beneficiary of the artificial intelligence (AI) trend, reported record revenues and issued upbeat forward guidance.

Similarly, Camtek Ltd. (CAMT) exhibited improving fundamentals due to its exposure to the AI infrastructure build-out. CTS Corp. (CTS), a producer of sensors, connectivity components and actuators that are used in broad set of end markets, was another top performer. The stock had been under pressure for much of the year because of its sensitivity to the weak commercial vehicle end market, but we maintained the position on the view that it was very inexpensive relative to its underlying quality and growth. CTS shares rebounded in the fourth quarter, aiding the Fund’s performance.

Outside of technology, Vita Coco Co., Inc. (COCO)—a global leader in coconut water—was the top contributor to Fund performance behind better-than-expected earnings and increased forward guidance. Vita Coco has a supply- chain advantage thanks to management’s focus on establishing long-term contracts with suppliers, which we think can help provide a foundation for a multiyear growth trajectory. (Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

Outlook

We believe the recent market environment, while challenging, has provided the opportunity to position the portfolio in out-of-favor companies that we believe are poised for an upward re-rating in valuations. Our emphasis throughout the year has been on adding new Fallen Angels, our term for growth companies that have hit a bump in the road and whose shares have dropped into value territory. We’ve seen the Fallen Angel opportunity set open and close on a rolling basis over time and have positioned the Fund accordingly. In 2022, for instance, we were very active in deploying capital into Fallen Angels. While this approach initially weighed on results, it ultimately drove strong outperformance in 2023–24. It’s impossible to predict when market conditions will shift, of course, but we believe the allocation to Fallen Angels provides the foundation for improved results in the coming year.

As always, we remain focused on finding opportunities on a company-by-company basis. Rather than trying to assess the likely impact of external factors, we will continue striving to do what we do best: identifying undervalued, high-quality businesses. Our analysis shows that although the Fund has underperformed, the fundamentals of our portfolio holdings have remained firm. We believe this potentially helps set the stage for a period of outperformance once the popularity of speculative momentum stocks subsides.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

Sincerely, Austin Bone
